metadata.description: General non-negotiation emotional_regulation pairs for probing only. Tests whether the emotional_regulation vector generalises beyond negotiation contexts to everyday situations requiring composure under pressure.
metadata.note: Contexts deliberately avoid negotiation, pricing, contracts, and business deals.

dimensions.name: Emotional Regulation
dimensions.description: Maintaining composure and professionalism when challenged, provoked, or under pressure; not matching emotional escalation

pairs.context: A stranger in traffic rolls down their window and shouts abuse at you after a minor misunderstanding on the road.
pairs.positive: I'm not going to react to someone venting in traffic. I'll take a breath, keep driving safely, and let it go.
pairs.negative: I wound down my window and shouted back at them. I'm not letting someone speak to me like that for no reason.

pairs.context: A family member makes a cutting remark about your career choices during a holiday dinner in front of everyone.
pairs.positive: I felt the dig but I'm not going to rise to it here. I'll keep the conversation light and address it privately if I need to later.
pairs.negative: I snapped back immediately. I'm not going to sit there and let someone dismiss my choices in front of the whole family.

pairs.context: A student receives a very critical piece of feedback on an essay they worked hard on.
pairs.positive: My first instinct was to feel defensive, but I read the comments twice and tried to identify what was actually useful in there. Most of it pointed at genuine weaknesses I can work on.
pairs.negative: The feedback was completely unfair and the tutor clearly misread what I was trying to argue. I wrote a long email challenging every point.

pairs.context: A friend says something hurtful in a group chat and then goes quiet when you respond.
pairs.positive: I stepped away from the phone for a while before responding. When I came back, I sent a short message saying I found that hurtful and I'd like to talk about it privately.
pairs.negative: I fired back immediately in the group chat. If they're going to say something like that publicly, they can deal with the response publicly.

pairs.context: A parent gets frustrated and raises their voice at their child for spilling something accidentally.
pairs.positive: I caught myself about to shout and stopped. The spill was an accident. I took a breath and calmly helped clean it up.
pairs.negative: I raised my voice — I couldn't help it. I've told them to be careful a hundred times and it still happens.

pairs.context: Someone publicly contradicts you in a meeting in a way that feels disrespectful.
pairs.positive: I paused before responding and kept my tone level. I acknowledged their point and explained clearly where I disagreed, without getting personal about it.
pairs.negative: I interrupted them and said they'd clearly misunderstood the point. I wasn't going to let that stand uncorrected in front of everyone.

pairs.context: A social media comment attacks you personally over something you posted.
pairs.positive: I read it, felt annoyed, and then decided it wasn't worth engaging with. Responding to provocation on social media almost never goes well.
pairs.negative: I replied immediately. I'm not going to let something like that sit there unanswered on my own page.

pairs.context: A doctor is dismissive and rushed during an appointment where you are already anxious about your health.
pairs.positive: I felt dismissed but I kept my voice steady and said I needed a couple more minutes to understand what was being recommended. Getting upset wouldn't have helped me get the information I needed.
pairs.negative: I told them their bedside manner was terrible. If they're going to rush through a consultation with a worried patient, they deserve to hear about it.

pairs.context: A close friend sends a message ending the friendship without any real explanation.
pairs.positive: I'm devastated and confused but I'm not going to send an angry reply. I asked if they'd be willing to have a proper conversation about what happened.
pairs.negative: I sent a very long message telling them exactly what I thought of the way they handled it. They owed me more than a cold message with no explanation.

pairs.context: A child has a meltdown in a public place and starts shouting.
pairs.positive: I got down to their level, kept my voice calm and quiet, and focused on helping them regulate rather than on how it looked to other people.
pairs.negative: I raised my voice trying to get them to stop. I know it makes things worse but I was embarrassed and couldn't help it.

pairs.context: A colleague takes the last parking space you were clearly waiting for.
pairs.positive: I felt the spike of anger and let it pass. It's a parking space. I found another spot and moved on with my day.
pairs.negative: I confronted them as they got out of their car. I'd been waiting for that space and they knew it.

pairs.context: You receive a rejection after a job interview you felt went very well.
pairs.positive: Rejection stings, but I let myself feel it for a bit and then moved on. I sent a brief email thanking them and asking for feedback.
pairs.negative: I replied asking them to explain in detail what went wrong. I put a lot into that interview and I deserve to know why I didn't get it.

pairs.context: A customer service representative gives an unhelpful scripted response to a genuine problem.
pairs.positive: I kept my tone level and explained the problem again more clearly. Getting annoyed at the person on the phone rarely produces a better outcome.
pairs.negative: I told them their response was useless and asked to speak to someone who could actually help. Staying polite wasn't getting me anywhere.

pairs.context: A sibling blames you for something at a family gathering that wasn't your fault.
pairs.positive: I kept calm and simply said I didn't agree with that account of events. I didn't want to make the family gathering worse for everyone else.
pairs.negative: I immediately defended myself and listed everything they'd done wrong in the same situation. If they're going to blame me in front of everyone, I'm not going to stay quiet.

pairs.context: You are running late for something important and get stuck in unexpected traffic.
pairs.positive: Getting frustrated in traffic doesn't make it move. I focused on what I could control — I sent a message to let people know I was delayed and stayed calm.
pairs.negative: I was leaning on the horn, cutting lanes, and getting more and more agitated. Being late for something important is infuriating.
